Ripples of energy.<[silence:600ms]> Traveling without medium.<[silence:600ms]>

The phrase "light as a wave" comes from its behavior—spreading like ripples on water, yet needing no water to move.<[silence:600ms]> Sunlight, laser beams, even the glow of a phone screen all act this way:<[silence:200ms]> they bend around corners, overlap to create patterns, and stretch or compress like waves in a pond.<[silence:600ms]>

Here’s how it works:<[silence:200ms]> Light is made of electric and magnetic fields vibrating at right angles, pushing each other forward.<[silence:600ms]> Drop a pebble in water—the outward rings are waves.<[silence:600ms]> Now imagine those rings moving through empty space, undisturbed by wind or obstacles.<[silence:600ms]> This is how starlight reaches Earth after years of travel.<[silence:600ms]> When light waves meet, they combine:<[silence:200ms]> two crests align to brighten (like overlapping flashlight beams), or a crest and trough cancel out (explaining why some CDs shine rainbow colors—grooves act as tiny mirrors, bending waves into interference patterns).<[silence:600ms]>

Radio telescopes use this principle.<[silence:600ms]> They capture cosmic waves—some stretched as wide as mountains—from galaxies billions of light-years away, translating invisible ripples into images of the universe’s infancy.<[silence:600ms]>
Three related subjects are sound as waves, quantum particle duality, and how lenses bend light.<[silence:600ms]>
clean_story:
Ripples of energy. Traveling without medium.

The phrase "light as a wave" comes from its behavior—spreading like ripples on water, yet needing no water to move. Sunlight, laser beams, even the glow of a phone screen all act this way: they bend around corners, overlap to create patterns, and stretch or compress like waves in a pond.

Here’s how it works: Light is made of electric and magnetic fields vibrating at right angles, pushing each other forward. Drop a pebble in water—the outward rings are waves. Now imagine those rings moving through empty space, undisturbed by wind or obstacles. This is how starlight reaches Earth after years of travel. When light waves meet, they combine: two crests align to brighten (like overlapping flashlight beams), or a crest and trough cancel out (explaining why some CDs shine rainbow colors—grooves act as tiny mirrors, bending waves into interference patterns).

Radio telescopes use this principle. They capture cosmic waves—some stretched as wide as mountains—from galaxies billions of light-years away, translating invisible ripples into images of the universe’s infancy.
Three related subjects are sound as waves, quantum particle duality, and how lenses bend light.
